description: To remain genuinely unsettled about whether a proposition is true or well enough supported. Doubt can be slight or deep, and it stops well short of denying a claim or refuting it; it is a suspended judgment, not a verdict.
sound_symbolism: Rounded, unvoiced 'wha' begins as breath moving through an uncertain opening. Closed lips hold the stress on 'mo' inside a dark round chamber. After it, a clean hiatus separates 'o' from bright 'i'. The held middle steps across one audible suspension into light.
grammatical_notes: A transitive verb with a nominal object or declarative complement: 'mia mena ha remuma kithela nai meno whamoi.' (I doubt that this conclusion is valid.) The event-noun rule supplies doubt. Degree descriptors may modify the verb. Distinct from 'ma nohero' (not believe), which may simply report absence of belief, and from 'whekate' (refute), which claims to show failure.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Doubt can be held as attentive uncertainty without demanding immediate attachment to affirmation or denial.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A speaker can disclose uncertainty directly, reducing pressure to perform certainty or turn hesitation into covert disagreement.

semantic_domains.cognition: an unsettled stance toward a proposition or its support
semantic_domains.wisdom: recognition that judgment may remain open while inquiry continues
